1-genital-changes.explain: The non-pregnant uterus weighs 50 g and increases to 1000 g at term.

1-genital-changes.q: The uterine volume at term is approximately:
1-genital-changes.explain: Uterine volume increases from 10 mL (non-pregnant) to 5000 mL at term.

1-genital-changes.q: Myometrial hypertrophy is primarily due to:
1-genital-changes.explain: Hypertrophy of the myometrium is an estrogenic effect, while hyperplasia is a progesterone effect.

1-genital-changes.q: The lower uterine segment starts to form at:
1-genital-changes.explain: The lower uterine segment (from isthmus + lower 1/2 inch of uterine body) starts forming at 12 weeks and reaches 10 cm at full term.

options: Painless contractions that may cause discomfort late in pregnancy
Contractions that only occur in multiple pregnancies
1-genital-changes.explain: Braxton Hicks are irregular, painless contractions that cause discomfort late in pregnancy and may account for false labor pains.

1-genital-changes.q: Uterine blood flow at term reaches approximately:
1-genital-changes.explain: Uterine artery lumen is doubled and blood flow increases to about 500 mL/min at term.

2-hematological-changes.q: Blood volume increases during pregnancy by approximately:
2-hematological-changes.explain: Blood volume increases gradually by 30–40% above non-pregnant level, reaching maximum at 32 weeks.

2-hematological-changes.q: Physiological anemia of pregnancy results from:
options: Plasma volume increase exceeding red cell mass increase
2-hematological-changes.explain: Plasma volume increases by 45%, while red cell mass increases by only 30%, causing hemodilution (physiological anemia).

2-hematological-changes.q: WBC count during pregnancy can reach up to:
2-hematological-changes.explain: WBCs increase from 7,000/mm³ to 10,500/mm³ in pregnancy, and up to 16,000/mm³ during labor.

2-hematological-changes.q: Which clotting factor is tripled during pregnancy?
2-hematological-changes.explain: Factor VIII is tripled. Fibrinogen is nearly doubled, and Factors VII and X are doubled.

2-hematological-changes.q: Pregnancy is considered a hypercoagulable state due to all EXCEPT:
2-hematological-changes.explain: Pregnancy is hypercoagulable due to increased clotting factors AND decreased fibrinolytic activity, which together increase VTE risk.

3-cardiovascular-system.q: Cardiac output reaches its maximum increase of 40% above non-pregnant level at:
3-cardiovascular-system.explain: Cardiac output increases to reach its maximum of 40% above non-pregnant level at 20 weeks, then remains constant till term.

3-cardiovascular-system.q: Cardiac output at term is approximately:
3-cardiovascular-system.explain: Cardiac output at term is approximately 6.5 L/min, due to increased stroke volume and heart rate.

3-cardiovascular-system.q: Despite increased cardiac output, blood pressure decreases in pregnancy due to:
3-cardiovascular-system.explain: Peripheral resistance is decreased due to peripheral vasodilation caused by increased progesterone, leading to lower BP despite increased COP.

3-cardiovascular-system.q: During which stage of labor does cardiac output increase the most?
3-cardiovascular-system.explain: COP increases more during the second stage of labor due to uterine contractions and bearing-down efforts.

4-respiratory-system.q: Tidal volume increases during pregnancy by approximately:
4-respiratory-system.explain: Tidal volume increases by about 40% throughout pregnancy.

4-respiratory-system.q: Hyperventilation in pregnancy is primarily due to:
options: Increased sensitivity of respiratory center to progesterone
4-respiratory-system.explain: Hyperventilation results from increased tidal volume due to increased sensitivity of the respiratory center to excess progesterone.

4-respiratory-system.q: The diaphragm is displaced upward by the enlarged uterus by approximately:
4-respiratory-system.explain: The enlarged uterus displaces the diaphragm up to 4 cm, reducing diaphragmatic mobility.

5-urinary-system.q: Renal blood flow and GFR increase during pregnancy by approximately:
5-urinary-system.explain: Renal blood flow and GFR increase by 50%, leading to decreased creatinine and urea.

5-urinary-system.q: Renal glucosuria in pregnancy results from:
5-urinary-system.explain: Increased excretion of glucose due to reduced renal glucose threshold leads to renal glucosuria.

5-urinary-system.q: Ureteric dilatation during pregnancy is more pronounced on the right side due to:
5-urinary-system.explain: The right ureter is more dilated due to dextrorotation of the uterus pressing against the pelvic brim.

6-gastrointestinal-tract.q: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (emesis gravidarum) typically:
options: Starts at 2 weeks, improves by 8 weeks
Starts between 4–6 weeks, improves by about 12 weeks
Starts at 8 weeks, continues until delivery
Starts at 12 weeks, improves by 20 weeks
6-gastrointestinal-tract.explain: Nausea (morning sickness) starts between 4th and 6th weeks and improves by about the 12th week.

6-gastrointestinal-tract.q: Heartburn in pregnancy is primarily caused by:
options: Relaxing effect of progesterone on the esophageal sphincter
6-gastrointestinal-tract.explain: Heartburn is due to reflux of acidic gastric contents caused by the relaxing effect of progesterone on the esophageal sphincter.

6-gastrointestinal-tract.q: Which GI change during pregnancy is NOT primarily caused by progesterone-induced smooth muscle relaxation?
6-gastrointestinal-tract.explain: Gingivitis is due to increased vascularity and hypertrophy of interdental papillae, not smooth muscle relaxation. Heartburn, constipation, piles, and ureteric dilatation are all progesterone-driven.

6-gastrointestinal-tract.q: The appendix during pregnancy is displaced:
6-gastrointestinal-tract.explain: The appendix is displaced upward and laterally by the enlarging uterus.

7-metabolic-changes.q: Total weight gain during pregnancy is approximately:
7-metabolic-changes.explain: Weight gain in pregnancy is increased by approximately 10–12 kg.

7-metabolic-changes.q: Pregnancy is considered diabetogenic due to:
7-metabolic-changes.explain: Pregnancy hormones create an anti-insulin effect, making pregnancy diabetogenic with mild fasting hypoglycemia and postprandial hyperglycemia.

7-metabolic-changes.q: Water and sodium retention in pregnancy is due to:
7-metabolic-changes.explain: There is a tendency to water and sodium retention due to increased aldosterone.

8-musculoskeletal-changes.q: Musculoskeletal changes in pregnancy include all EXCEPT:
8-musculoskeletal-changes.explain: Changes include progressive softening of pelvic joints, progressive lordosis, and foot flattening, not increased bone density.

9-endocrine-changes.q: Which anterior pituitary cells increase during pregnancy?
9-endocrine-changes.explain: The anterior pituitary shows hypertrophy with an increased number of prolactin-secreting cells.

9-endocrine-changes.q: Thyroid function in pregnancy shows all EXCEPT:
9-endocrine-changes.explain: Pregnancy shows increased TBG, total T3/T4, and BMR, but TSH is decreased (not increased).

9-endocrine-changes.q: The parathyroid gland hypertrophies in pregnancy due to:
9-endocrine-changes.explain: Parathyroid hypertrophy occurs with increased demand for calcium during pregnancy.

10-skin-changes.q: Palmar erythema during pregnancy is due to:
10-skin-changes.explain: Palmar erythema is due to increased estrogen.

options: Pigmentation in the midline below the umbilicus
10-skin-changes.explain: Linea nigra is pigmentation in the midline below the umbilicus.
Chloasma gravidarum is pigmentation on the face during pregnancy.

11-breast-changes.q: Montgomery tubercles appear during which month of pregnancy?
11-breast-changes.explain: Montgomery tubercles (hypertrophy of the orifices of lacteal ducts) appear during the second month alongside increased pigmentation of the nipple and primary areola.
Colostrum secretion begins during the third month of pregnancy.
The secondary areola (pigmented area around the primary areola) appears during the fifth month.

11-breast-changes.q: Symptoms of tingling to frank pain in the breasts (mastodynia) occur in which month?
11-breast-changes.explain: Mastodynia, along with increased size and vascularity, occurs in the first month of pregnancy.
